toolUseResult.newString: admin-session-start
toolUseResult.originalFile:
{
  "id": "netadmin-dean-ollama-gpu-setup",
  "name": "Dean — Ollama GPU Setup on Ubuntu VM with Open WebUI",
  "date": "2026-04-04",
  "persona": "Dean",
  "skills_activated": ["net-session-start", "sysadmin-triage", "service-deployment"],
  "topic": "Install Nvidia drivers, Nvidia Container Toolkit, Ollama with GPU acceleration, and Open WebUI on Ubuntu 24.04 VM with GTX 1650",
  "outcome": "resolved",
  "turns": [
    {"role": "user", "content": "I have a gpu in my ubuntu vm, what services can I use that will benefit from the gpu power. GTX 1660 4gb"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "A GTX 1660 in a VM is solid for several self-hosted workloads. What Ubuntu version, and is GPU passthrough already working? Does nvidia-smi show the card? Top services: Jellyfin/Plex for hardware transcoding, Ollama for local LLMs, Stable Diffusion for image generation, Frigate NVR for camera object detection, Handbrake for batch video encoding."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "I would like to set up ollama"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Great choice. What's your Ubuntu version, do you have Docker installed, and does nvidia-smi show the GTX 1660?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "ubuntu 24lts, docker is installed, i dont know what nvidia-smi is or how it works"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "nvidia-smi checks if Ubuntu can see your GPU. Run nvidia-smi. If it shows a table with the GPU, drivers are installed. If command not found, we need to install Nvidia drivers first."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Command nvidia-smi not found, but can be installed with various nvidia-utils packages"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Drivers aren't installed. Run sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y nvidia-driver-570. After it finishes, reboot with sudo reboot. Then run nvidia-smi again. To revert: sudo apt remove nvidia-driver-570."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "nvidia-smi now shows GPU running — NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650, 4096MiB, CUDA 12.8"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "GPU is working — it's a GTX 1650 not 1660, same 4GB VRAM. Now install Nvidia Container Toolkit so Docker can access the GPU, then run Ollama container with --gpus all flag."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "E: Unable to locate package nvidia-container-toolkit"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "The repo didn't add correctly. Provided step-by-step commands to add the Nvidia container toolkit repo manually using distribution variable, GPG key, and repo list."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Ollama container is now running on port 11434"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Run docker exec ollama ollama run llama3.2 to pull and test a model. Check nvidia-smi while it's generating to confirm GPU usage."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "ollama is not in the nvidia-smi process list"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "It only shows during active generation. Send a message in the chat, then check nvidia-smi. If GPU memory stays at 4MiB, check docker logs ollama | grep -i gpu."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Logs show: inference compute CUDA0 NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 3.6 GiB available"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Working perfectly. Ollama detected the GPU via CUDA with 3.6GB usable VRAM. Models run on GPU automatically."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "I want to connect ollama to a web server now"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Open WebUI is the best option. Run the container with OLLAMA_BASE_URL pointing to host.docker.internal:11434, exposed on port 3000."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Server is running but nothing shows up, status shows health: starting"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "It takes 1-2 minutes on first launch. Wait, then check docker ps again — once status changes to healthy, refresh the browser."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Open WebUI is running, how to connect the GPU model?"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Already connected via OLLAMA_BASE_URL. Check model dropdown at top of chat. If no models appear, verify the Ollama URL in Admin Panel > Settings > Connections."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "URL is correct but no model shows up in dropdown"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Check docker exec ollama ollama list. If empty, pull the model again with docker exec ollama ollama pull llama3.2. Then refresh in Open WebUI connections settings."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Model now shows up. How can I give it memory so it becomes a personal AI for my equipment?"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Three options: System Prompt per conversation, Knowledge base in Workspace for uploaded docs, or create a custom Modelfile with baked-in context using ollama create my-admin."}
  ]
}

{
  "id": "netadmin-tyler-nextcloud-cloudflare-tunnel",
  "name": "Tyler — Nextcloud Behind Cloudflare Tunnel 502 Error",
  "date": "2026-04-04",
  "persona": "Tyler",
  "skills_activated": ["net-session-start", "reverse-proxy-advisor"],
  "topic": "Nextcloud accessible locally but returns 502 Bad Gateway through Cloudflare Tunnel",
  "outcome": "resolved",
  "turns": [
    {"role": "user", "content": "I set up Nextcloud in Docker on my Ubuntu server and it works fine at 192.168.1.50:8080, but when I try to access it through my Cloudflare Tunnel at cloud.mydomain.com I get a 502 Bad Gateway error."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Classic tunnel-to-container issue. What Ubuntu version are you on, and is cloudflared running on the same host as Docker or separately? What's the tunnel config pointing to — localhost:8080 or the host IP?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Ubuntu 24.04 LTS server. cloudflared is running as a systemd service on the same host as Docker. The tunnel is pointing to http://localhost:8080. I set it up through the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Good — Ubuntu 24.04, same host. Check two things. First, SSL mode in Cloudflare dashboard — set it to Full, not Full (Strict). Second, verify cloudflared can reach Nextcloud: run `curl -I http://localhost:8080` on the server. What do you get?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "curl gives me HTTP/1.1 200 OK with some Nextcloud headers. SSL mode was set to Full (Strict) which I changed to Full in the Cloudflare dashboard. Waited a couple minutes but still getting 502 Bad Gateway in the browser."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "SSL change can take a minute to propagate. Also check: in your Nextcloud config.php, is your tunnel domain in the trusted_domains array? Without it, Nextcloud rejects the request. Find it at your Nextcloud volume path, usually something like `docker exec nextcloud cat /var/www/html/config/config.php | grep trusted`."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Found it — the trusted_domains array in config.php only has localhost and 192.168.1.50 listed. The tunnel domain cloud.mydomain.com is not in there. How do I add it without breaking the existing entries?"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Run: `docker exec -u www-data nextcloud php occ config:system:set trusted_domains 2 --value=cloud.mydomain.com`. Then test cloud.mydomain.com in your browser. You should see the Nextcloud login. If it still fails, check `docker logs nextcloud --tail 20` for errors. To revert, run the same command with `--value=` to clear it."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "That fixed it! Nextcloud loads through the tunnel now."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Good. Verify: open an incognito window to cloud.mydomain.com and confirm login works. Then enable WebSockets in your tunnel settings (Zero Trust > tunnel > public hostname > Connection) — Nextcloud uses them for real-time file updates. Verify WebSockets by uploading a file and confirming it appears in real time on another device. Set a strong admin password if you haven't — this is now internet-facing."}
  ]
}

{
  "id": "netadmin-raj-jellyfin-docker-transcoding",
  "name": "Raj — Jellyfin Docker Hardware Transcoding Not Working",
  "date": "2026-04-04",
  "persona": "Raj",
  "skills_activated": ["net-session-start", "service-deployment"],
  "topic": "Jellyfin Docker container starts but hardware transcoding fails with permission errors on Intel GPU",
  "outcome": "resolved",
  "turns": [
    {"role": "user", "content": "I'm running Jellyfin in Docker on Ubuntu 24.04 but hardware transcoding isn't working. The container starts fine and I can browse my library, but when I try to play something that needs transcoding it just buffers forever."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Sounds like the GPU isn't accessible inside the container. What Ubuntu version, what GPU — Intel iGPU, Nvidia, or AMD? And can you share your docker-compose.yml or the docker run command you used?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Ubuntu 24.04. Intel i5-8500 so it has Intel UHD 630 iGPU. Here's my compose:\nservices:\n  jellyfin:\n    image: jellyfin/jellyfin\n    ports:\n      - 8096:8096\n    volumes:\n      - ./config:/config\n      - /media:/media"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "The compose file is missing GPU passthrough. First, verify the render device exists on the host: `ls -la /dev/dri/`. You should see `renderD128` and `card0`. What's the output?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "I see renderD128 and card0. renderD128 shows group 'render' with permissions crw-rw----"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Good, the GPU is there. Two things needed. First, add the device to your compose file under the jellyfin service:\n```\n    devices:\n      - /dev/dri/renderD128:/dev/dri/renderD128\n    group_add:\n      - \"render\"\n```\nThen run `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. After it starts, verify the device is visible inside: `docker exec jellyfin ls -la /dev/dri/`."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Added that and restarted. The device shows up inside the container now. But transcoding still fails — Jellyfin logs show 'Failed to initialize VAAPI'."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "The host needs the Intel VAAPI driver so the container can access GPU acceleration. Run `sudo apt install intel-media-va-driver` — this installs the video acceleration driver, non-destructive and safe to remove later with `sudo apt remove intel-media-va-driver`. Then restart: `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. Verify with `docker exec jellyfin /usr/lib/jellyfin-ffmpeg/vainfo` — it should list VA-API entrypoints."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Installed the driver, restarted, and vainfo shows entrypoints now. Transcoding is working — I can see it using the GPU in htop. Thanks!"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Nice. In Jellyfin go to Dashboard > Playback > Transcoding, select VAAPI as acceleration, set device to /dev/dri/renderD128. Enable hardware decoding for H.264, HEVC, and VP9. Verify by playing a file that requires transcoding — check Dashboard > Active Streams shows '(HW)' next to the transcode. If transcoding breaks later, revert by setting acceleration back to 'None' in the same menu."}
  ]
}

{
  "id": "netadmin-grace-nextcloud-502-after-upgrade",
  "name": "Grace — Nextcloud 502 After Unattended Apt Upgrade",
  "date": "2026-04-04",
  "persona": "Grace",
  "skills_activated": ["net-session-start", "sysadmin-triage"],
  "topic": "Nextcloud returning 502 errors after unattended-upgrades ran overnight and updated PHP",
  "outcome": "resolved",
  "turns": [
    {"role": "user", "content": "My Nextcloud server is showing 502 Bad Gateway since this morning. My employees can't access their files. Nothing was changed — it was working fine yesterday."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Let's get this back up. What OS and version is the server running? Is Nextcloud installed natively with Apache/Nginx, or in Docker? Can you SSH into the server right now?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Ubuntu 22.04, installed with Apache following a blog guide about 6 months ago. Yes I can SSH in."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Check if Apache is running: `systemctl status apache2`. Also check if anything updated overnight: `cat /var/log/unattended-upgrades/unattended-upgrades.log | tail -30`. What do you see?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Apache is active and running. The upgrade log shows it updated php8.1-fpm and a few other php packages last night at 3am."},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "PHP-FPM got updated and likely needs a restart. Run `sudo systemctl restart php8.1-fpm` — this restarts the PHP processor that Apache uses to serve Nextcloud. It's non-destructive and only takes a second. Then check status: `systemctl status php8.1-fpm`. Try loading Nextcloud after. If it makes things worse, revert with `sudo systemctl stop php8.1-fpm && sudo systemctl start php8.1-fpm`."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "That brought it back! Employees can access files again. But how do I stop this from happening again?"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Open `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades` with `sudo nano /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades` — this edits the auto-update config. Find `Unattended-Upgrade::Package-Blacklist` and add `\"php*\";` to block PHP from auto-updating. Verify with `sudo unattended-upgrade --dry-run` — PHP packages should not appear. To revert, remove the `\"php*\";` line. You'll manually run `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` for PHP when ready, then restart php8.1-fpm after."}
  ]
}

Before I do -- a few things I need to know: 1. pfSense version and is it running as a VM on Proxmox, or on bare metal? 2. Proxmox version and how many NICs does the host have? 3. What VLANs are you planning? 4. Current setup -- is the switch factory-reset, or did the previous owner leave a config on it?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "1) 2.8.1-RELEASE (amd64) 2)Virtual Environment 9.1.1 3) I have vlan 20 (192.168.2.1) and vlan 30 (192.168.3.1) and just LAN (192.168.1.1)"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Good info. Which of the 3 NIC ports are currently in use, and what's each one doing? For example: Port 1 WAN, Port 2 LAN, Port 3 unused? And are VLANs 20 and 30 already configured in pfSense and working, or are those still to be created?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Port 1: wan port 2: lan port3: eerowifi"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Got it. So VLANs 20 and 30 -- are those already configured as VLAN interfaces in pfSense, or do we need to create them from scratch? And which port on pfSense will connect to the Cisco switch? Are you planning to trunk VLANs over the LAN port (port 2), or use port 3, or add the switch differently?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "The vlans are already configured, pfsnese will use the port 2 for (vlan 20) and port 3(vlan 30)"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "So Port 2 carries LAN untagged plus VLAN 20 tagged, and Port 3 carries Eero untagged plus VLAN 30 tagged. Is that right? This matters for how we configure the Cisco switch ports -- specifically whether the uplink ports to pfSense need to be trunk ports (carrying both tagged and untagged traffic) or access ports (one VLAN per port)."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Yes, this is correct"},
    {"role": "agent", "content": "Here's your change plan. Goal: Integrate Cisco SG200-48P switch with pfSense (Proxmox VM) to carry LAN, VLAN 20, and VLAN 30 across switch ports. Phase 1: Access the switch at 192.168.1.254, change default password. Phase 2: Create VLANs 20 and 30 on the switch. Phase 3: Configure trunk ports -- port to pfSense port 2 gets native VLAN 1 plus VLAN 20 tagged, port to pfSense port 3 gets native VLAN 1 plus VLAN 30 tagged. Phase 4: Assign access ports per VLAN. Phase 5: Save config, cable up, verify each VLAN gets correct DHCP and can ping gateway and internet. Rollback: disconnect switch, revert to direct cabling."},

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What This Repo Is

A teaching artifact for COS 598/498 (Generative AI Agents, Spring 2026, University of Maine). It contains a **Network Admin Agent** prototyped using Claude Code skills — including the skills themselves, test personas, experiment logs, and a Python evaluation pipeline.

The agent helps network administrators safely manage pfSense firewalls, servers, VPNs, and DNS through structured triage, change planning, and validation. It is not a production system. The purpose is to demonstrate and teach AI agent prototyping.

## Running the Evaluation Pipeline

```bash
# Demo mode — no API key needed, uses precomputed scores
python eval/run_eval.py --precomputed

# Interactive mode — prompts you to score conversations yourself
python eval/run_eval.py

# No external dependencies required; runs on Python 3.10+ standard library
```

To add a new conversation to evaluate, append an entry to `eval/sample_conversations.json` following the schema in `eval/README.md`, then run `python eval/run_eval.py`.

## Architecture

### Agent Skills (`.claude/skills/`)

The agent is implemented entirely as Claude Code skills — markdown files with YAML frontmatter and prompt instructions. There is no application code. Skills chain to each other via slash commands.

| Skill | File | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `net-session-start` | `.claude/skills/net-session-start/SKILL.md` | Router — classifies network admin intent and dispatches to the appropriate skill |
| `pfsense-triage` | `.claude/skills/pfsense-triage/SKILL.md` | Impact-first triage for pfSense and network incidents |
| `dns-troubleshooter` | `.claude/skills/dns-troubleshooter/SKILL.md` | Structured DNS debugging (resolution chain, cache, forwarders, split-horizon) |
| `vpn-advisor` | `.claude/skills/vpn-advisor/SKILL.md` | IPsec, OpenVPN, and WireGuard configuration guidance with validation |
| `network-change-planner` | `.claude/skills/network-change-planner/SKILL.md` | Safe change planning with prerequisites, risk controls, rollback |
| `log-analyzer` | `.claude/skills/log-analyzer/SKILL.md` | Parse pfSense, syslog, and Windows Event Log for patterns and errors |
| `server-hardening-advisor` | `.claude/skills/server-hardening-advisor/SKILL.md` | Practical hardening for pfSense-adjacent Linux/Windows servers |
| `backup-and-recovery` | `.claude/skills/backup-and-recovery/SKILL.md` | Backup verification, restore testing, and disaster recovery planning |
| `monitoring-setup` | `.claude/skills/monitoring-setup/SKILL.md` | SNMP, Zabbix/LibreNMS/Nagios configuration with alert thresholds |
| `documentation-generator` | `.claude/skills/documentation-generator/SKILL.md` | Generates change records, incident reports, and runbook entries |
| `net-session-close` | `.claude/skills/net-session-close/SKILL.md` | Session closer — decision summary, action checklist, verification reminders, saves conversation log |

Routing logic lives in `net-session-start`. Network admin skills follow a safety-first pattern: triage impact, confirm environment, gather evidence, recommend lowest-risk fix, include validation steps, and provide rollback procedures.

### Personas (`data/personas/`)

JSON files representing test users for network admin scenarios. Skills are designed to read persona context silently — they never reference the file directly to the user.

| Persona | File | Profile | Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tamara Wells | `tamara.json` | School district IT coordinator, self-taught | DHCP scope exhaustion before state testing |
| Marco Reyes | `marco.json` | MSP junior tech, 18 months in | IPsec VPN tunnel dropping every 30-60 min |
| Diane Olsen | `diane.json` | Plant IT manager, inherited undocumented network | New VLAN for production floor equipment |
| Yusuf Abdi | `yusuf.json` | Non-profit IT, zero budget, wears all hats | Captive portal broken after pfSense upgrade |
| Chen Wei | `chen_wei.json` | Hospital network engineer, HIPAA compliance | IoT medical device segmentation for audit |

### Saved Conversations (`data/conversations/`)

JSON logs of completed network admin sessions, saved automatically by `net-session-close`. Each file contains the full turn-by-turn conversation in the same format as `eval/sample_conversations.json`, so conversations can be moved into the eval pipeline directly. Files are named `YYYY-MM-DD-<topic-slug>.json`.

### Experiments (`experiments/`)

Markdown logs of test sessions and iteration plans. Naming convention: `<persona>-<session#>.md` for conversation logs, `<persona>-<session#>-plan-for-<change>.md` for planned skill updates.

### Evaluation Pipeline (`eval/`)

Pure Python, no external dependencies:

- `run_eval.py` — entry point; handles CLI args and output formatting
- `evaluate.py` — orchestrator; loads conversations, runs metrics, collects scores
- `metrics.py` — structural metric functions (question ratio, response length, harmful pattern detection, etc.)
- `rubrics.py` — rubric definitions with 5 dimensions (safety, accuracy, triage quality, completeness, tone) plus precomputed scores
- `sample_conversations.json` — test dataset (~17 conversations: beneficial + unhelpful examples)

Conversations with IDs prefixed `netadmin-` automatically use the network admin metrics and rubrics. The structural metrics include: question ratio, response length, word ratio, first turn question, command safety, environment tracking, validation steps, and harmful patterns.

Results are written to `eval/results/`.

## Key Conventions

- **Skills must live in `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`** — not `skills/`, not `.claude/commands/`. Claude Code will not find them otherwise.
- **Each skill must have a "Critical Rules" section** defining what the agent must never do. Without it, the LLM defaults to generic helpful-assistant behavior.
- **Commit after each iteration** — the git history documents the design process.
- **Experiments go in `experiments/`** — one file per test session or planned change.
- When updating a skill based on experiment findings, use the plan file in `experiments/` as the spec and verify all existing exercises are preserved.

### Network Admin Agent Design Principles

- **Safety first**: Never suggest destructive commands without rollback. Confirm access levels before providing CLI commands.
- **Environment before advice**: Ask about pfSense version, OS, and access method in the first response. Commands differ across versions.
- **Validation is mandatory**: Every change recommendation must include specific verification steps (ping, nslookup, test connectivity, check logs).
- **Concise responses**: Keep agent turns under 100 words. Balance questions and instructions — aim for 40-67% of turns containing a question (avoids both lecturing and interrogation).
- **Word ratio discipline**: Agent should not dominate the conversation. Target < 2.0x user word count for beneficial interactions.
- **Rollback always**: Every change must have an explicit revert path. "Delete the rule you just added" is better than "undo the change."

## Prototyping Process

The full step-by-step process is documented in `docs/process-guide.md`. The implementation plan that produced this repo's structure is in `docs/implementation-plan.md`.

Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\deanh\Downloads\s26_3_31_class_and_guide-main\s26_3_31_class_and_guide-main\.claude\skills\admin-session-start

# Admin Agent -- Session Router

You are a network and systems operations assistant. You help with pfSense, server administration, self-hosted services, Docker, reverse proxies, and general infrastructure management.

## On Every New Conversation

1. Read the user message and classify intent.
2. Route to one skill using slash command.
3. If intent is unclear, ask one clarifying question.

## Intent Routing

### Network / pfSense
- Incident/outage/network troubleshooting -> `/pfsense-triage`
- Planned change/migration/network configuration -> `/network-change-planner`
- DNS resolution issues/debugging -> `/dns-troubleshooter`
- VPN setup/configuration/debugging -> `/vpn-advisor`
- Monitoring/alerting/SNMP setup -> `/monitoring-setup`

### System Admin / Servers
- Server issue/service crash/performance problem -> `/sysadmin-triage`
- Deploy/install/configure self-hosted service (Plex, Nextcloud, Ollama, Docker, etc.) -> `/service-deployment`
- Reverse proxy/Cloudflare Tunnel/Nginx/Caddy/Traefik/external access -> `/reverse-proxy-advisor`
- Security hardening/baseline/compliance -> `/server-hardening-advisor`
- Log interpretation/analysis -> `/log-analyzer`

### General
- Backup/restore/disaster recovery -> `/backup-and-recovery`
- Generate documentation/runbook/change record -> `/documentation-generator`
- Wrap-up, recap, handoff, next steps -> `/net-session-close`

## Multi-Intent Handling

If the user's message contains multiple intents (e.g., "DNS is broken AND I need to set up Plex"):

1. Acknowledge both intents explicitly.
2. Prioritize by urgency: incidents first, then planned changes, then advisory.
3. Route to the highest-priority skill first.
4. After the first skill completes, remind the user of the second intent and route to the next skill.
5. Example: "I see two things here — a DNS outage (urgent) and a Plex install (planned). Let's fix DNS first, then set up Plex."

## Operating Principles

- Safety before speed.
- Ask for environment details before specific commands.
- Prefer reversible actions first.
- State assumptions clearly.

## Response Style

- **Keep responses under 80 words** unless the user asks for detail. Concise answers respect the user's time.
- **Your first response must include a question.** Acknowledge what the user said, then ask what you need to proceed. Never open with a monologue.
- **Confirm environment in the first exchange.** Before giving any commands, confirm: OS/platform, version, and access level (GUI, SSH, console). A wrong-OS command wastes time and erodes trust.
- **Balance questions and answers.** Don't stack 4+ questions in a row (interrogation). Don't go 3+ turns without asking one (lecturing). Aim for one question per response, woven naturally into your answer.
- **Commands must be copy-paste ready.** No smart quotes, no markdown artifacts, no line wrapping that breaks in terminal. One command per code block when commands are long.

## Critical Rules

- Never present uncertain commands as facts.
- Never suggest destructive changes without rollback steps.
- Never claim to have executed actions unless user confirms execution.
- Never bypass authentication, policy, or legal constraints.
The router is loaded. What do you need help with?

message.content: I just setup a ollam server running on my server at 192.168.1.225 I have a gpu gtx 1650 4gb, how to I I create an ai agent using ollam that will monitor my pihole network?

content.text:
Interesting project — that's a couple of things combined. I see two intents here:

1. **Deploy an AI agent using Ollama** — service setup
2. **Monitor your Pi-hole network** — monitoring/alerting

Let me start with the deployment side via `/service-deployment`.

Before I lay out a plan — a few questions:

1. Is Pi-hole already running, and where? (Same server, separate device, Docker?)
2. Is Ollama already running on 192.168.1.225, or do we need to set that up too?
3. What do you mean by "monitor" — watching DNS query logs, blocking stats, alerting on anomalies, or something else?

message.content: Pihole is already running on differnt server at dhauser@192.168.1.12 password: 1000 Ollam is running on 192.168.1.225:11434 and http://192.168.1.225:3000 for web ui. I want to create a script that checks all devices and what websites they have been on in the past 1 hour.

content.text:
Got it — Pi-hole's query log is the data source, and Ollama can analyze it. But first:

**Important: don't share passwords in chat.** I'll use placeholder references instead.

Here's the approach — a script on your Pi-hole server that:
1. Pulls the last hour of DNS queries from Pi-hole's API
2. Sends them to Ollama for analysis/summary

**Step 1 — Test Pi-hole API access.** SSH into your Pi-hole and run:

```
curl -s "http://localhost/admin/api.php?getAllQueries&from=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)&to=$(date +%s)&auth=YOUR_API_TOKEN"
```

You can find your API token in Pi-hole Admin > Settings > API > Show API token.

Does that return a JSON list of queries? Once we confirm the data source works, I'll build the script that feeds it to Ollama for analysis.


Once you're in the Linux shell on the Pi-hole, then run:

```
curl -s "http://localhost/admin/api.php?getAllQueries&from=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)&to=$(date +%s)&auth=YOUR_API_TOKEN"
```

content.text:
Pi-hole v6 changed the API path. Let's try the new endpoint:

```
curl -s "http://localhost/api/queries?from=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)&to=$(date +%s)" -H "X-FTL-SID: $(curl -s -X POST http://localhost/api/auth -d '{"password":"YOUR_PIHOLE_WEB_PASSWORD"}' | grep -o '"sid":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)"
```

Actually, that's messy. Let's do it in two steps:

**Step 1 — Get a session token:**

```
curl -s -X POST http://localhost/api/auth -d '{"password":"YOUR_PIHOLE_WEB_PASSWORD"}'
```

What does that return? It should give you a `sid` value.

content.text:
Got it. Now use that `sid` to pull the last hour of queries:

```
curl -s "http://localhost/api/queries?from=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)&to=$(date +%s)" -H "X-FTL-SID: Sb6HCXlzC8hgmhB3yeISbw="
```

Does it return a list of DNS queries?

content.text:
The API works. I can see queries from devices across your VLANs — Spotify, Apple, Roku, Wyze, Fubo, ZeroTier, etc. Now let's build the script.

Create this file on your Pi-hole server:

```
nano ~/pihole-report.sh
```

Paste this in:

```
#!/bin/bash

PIHOLE="http://localhost"
PASSWORD="YOUR_PIHOLE_WEB_PASSWORD"
OLLAMA="http://192.168.1.225:11434"

# Get auth token
SID=$(curl -s -X POST "$PIHOLE/api/auth" -d "{\"password\":\"$PASSWORD\"}" | grep -o '"sid":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)

# Get last hour of queries
FROM=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)
TO=$(date +%s)
QUERIES=$(curl -s "$PIHOLE/api/queries?from=$FROM&to=$TO" -H "X-FTL-SID: $SID")

# Summarize: unique domains per client IP
SUMMARY=$(echo "$QUERIES" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
clients = {}
for q in data.get('queries', []):
    ip = q['client']['ip']
    domain = q['domain']
    if q['type'] in ('A', 'AAAA', 'HTTPS'):
        clients.setdefault(ip, set()).add(domain)
for ip, domains in sorted(clients.items()):
    print(f'{ip}: {len(domains)} unique domains')
    for d in sorted(domains):
        print(f'  - {d}')
")

# Send to Ollama for analysis
curl -s "$OLLAMA/api/generate" -d "{
  \"model\": \"llama3.2\",
  \"prompt\": \"Analyze this DNS activity from the last hour. Flag anything unusual — suspicious domains, excessive queries, or tracking activity. Be concise.\n\n$SUMMARY\",
  \"stream\": false
}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['response'])"
```

Save and make it executable:

```
chmod +x ~/pihole-report.sh
```

Run it:

```
bash ~/pihole-report.sh
```

This pulls the queries, summarizes them by device, and sends the summary to Ollama for analysis. What does it output?

content.text:
You're right, sorry. The issue is the special characters and quotes getting mangled. Let me create the file differently.

On your Pi-hole server, run this to download the script directly:

```
cat > ~/pihole-report.sh << 'ENDOFSCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash

PIHOLE="http://localhost"
PASSWORD="YOUR_PIHOLE_WEB_PASSWORD"
OLLAMA="http://192.168.1.225:11434"

SID=$(curl -s -X POST "$PIHOLE/api/auth" -d "{\"password\":\"$PASSWORD\"}" | grep -o '"sid":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)

FROM=$(date -d '1 hour ago' +%s)
TO=$(date +%s)
QUERIES=$(curl -s "$PIHOLE/api/queries?from=$FROM&to=$TO" -H "X-FTL-SID: $SID")

SUMMARY=$(echo "$QUERIES" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
clients = {}
for q in data.get('queries', []):
    ip = q['client']['ip']
    domain = q['domain']
    if q['type'] in ('A', 'AAAA', 'HTTPS'):
        clients.setdefault(ip, set()).add(domain)
for ip, domains in sorted(clients.items()):
    print(f'{ip}: {len(domains)} unique domains')
    for d in sorted(domains):
        print(f'  - {d}')
")

curl -s "$OLLAMA/api/generate" -d "{\"model\": \"llama3.2\", \"prompt\": \"Analyze this DNS activity from the last hour. Flag anything unusual or suspicious. Be concise.\n\n$SUMMARY\", \"stream\": false}" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['response'])"
ENDOFSCRIPT
```

Then:

```
chmod +x ~/pihole-report.sh
```

Then edit the password:

```
nano ~/pihole-report.sh
```

Change `YOUR_PIHOLE_WEB_PASSWORD` to your actual password, save, and run:

```
bash ~/pihole-report.sh
```

Did the `cat` command work without errors?
